title: Adaptive Planning

content.question: Are you following a rigid plan past its usefulness?
sop: Set long-term objectives (annual) that state the destination, not the exact route.
Break execution into short cycles, monthly or quarterly, that can be revised without abandoning the destination.
Review actual results against the plan at the end of each cycle.
Update the next cycle's plan based on that feedback, not the original assumptions (see calculation, page 5).
Keep the long-term objective fixed unless the feedback says the destination itself was wrong.
questions: Are you following a rigid plan past its usefulness?
When did you last actually change a plan because of new information, versus just pushing through because you'd already committed to it?
What would you do differently this quarter if you were planning it today instead of when you originally wrote it?
content.equation_example: This quarter's target plus a Feedback Adjustment (based on actual results vs. expectations, positive or negative) equals next quarter's target. Plan 100 plus Feedback Adjustment negative 10 equals Plan 90. A plan that keeps adjusting toward reality is working; a plan that never changes despite missed targets means feedback isn't being used.
content.closing_statement: Hold the destination loosely enough to adjust the route.

caption:
Principle Nº 199: Adaptive Planning

Plan dynamically.

Ask yourself: Are you following a rigid plan past its usefulness?
